entities.name: vāda
entities.cite: Vadas — the philosophical systems, with their founders and their fortunes — asta yatesu, set like stars

entities.name: kartṛ
entities.cite: their authors pass, their schools scatter

entities.name: karma
entities.cite: the karma declared in sruti and smrti, the ritual and practical order

entities.cite: Yet the karma declared in sruti and smrti

entities.name: smṛti
entities.cite: Yet the karma declared in sruti and smrti

entities.name: loka
entities.cite: the world does not step across it

claims.cite: When the doctrines have set, and other authors are no more

claims.name: avyativartita
claims.cite: the world does not transgress the rite declared by sruti and smrti

rejected_readings.label: symmetrical_mortality
rejected_readings.rendering: When doctrines and their authors pass away, the world likewise abandons its practices.
rejected_readings.why: Inverts the asymmetry: the commentary insists doctrines are mortal but rites are not. This reading asserts that karma also perishes, contradicting the core observation.

rejected_readings.label: doctrine_dependent_ritual
rejected_readings.rendering: The ritual order exists only so long as the philosophical systems that originally justified it continue to be argued and defended.
rejected_readings.why: Treats karma as parasitic on vada-s. The commentary's point is that karma persists precisely because it is practice-borne, not doctrine-borne. This reading makes karma as mortal as the doctrines, contradicting the declared independence.

rejected_readings.label: practice_as_source
rejected_readings.rendering: The rituals establish themselves through constant practice, while śruti and smṛti merely record what the world has already established.
rejected_readings.why: Reverses the logical and textual priority. The verse and commentary make clear that it is the declaration in śruti and smṛti that grounds the karma, not the other way around. This reading asserts an unlicensed source relation (loka creates/authorizes karma) that the axioms do not warrant.

notes: Verse 1.133 grounds the persistence of ritual practice in the dual textual authority (śruti and smṛti) rather than in any doctrinal system. The commentary's asymmetry between mortal doctrines and persistent rites becomes the empirical correlate of 1.132's cosmological claim: the practice-bearing core endures across the rise and fall of philosophical schools. The contract does not encode the full cosmic scope but focuses on the observable historical phenomenon.
